question: Based on this image, what is the most likely diagnosis?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial CT image for key anatomic and contrast findings. I see a single, well-defined tubular structure in the mediastinum that is opacified with contrast; the airway (tracheal lumen) is seen separately and does not contain contrast. There is no obvious extraluminal contrast, no rim-enhancing fluid collection, and no dispersed air pocket around the tubular structure.

Next, I relate those observations to the diagnostic choices. A tubular, contrast-filled structure coursing through the mediastinum is most consistent with a surgically created intrathoracic conduit (option A). A tracheoesophageal fistula (B) would show contrast passing into the trachea/airway, which is not present. A mediastinal abscess (C) would appear as a localized fluid/air collection with surrounding inflammatory change, which is not seen. Intrathoracic colon interposition (D) would commonly show colonic morphology (haustral pattern or gas-fluid levels), not a smooth contrast-filled tube. Esophageal perforation with leak (E) would show extraluminal contrast or surrounding leak, which is absent.

Therefore, the imaging appearance—an isolated, smooth, contrast-opacified tubular structure in the mediastinum—best fits a tubular gastric conduit (gastric pull-up).
caption: Postoperative chest CT with gastrografin showed the gastric tubulization (arrow).
sub_caption: Postoperative chest CT with gastrografin showed the gastric tubulization (arrow).
image_context: The patient had a regular course of recovery; however, on the day 15 post-surgery, when the patient was switched to a semisolid diet, loss of food bolus from the pleural drainage tubes was observed. She then underwent a re-thoracotomy with detection of the fistula, closure of the leak, and affixation of a muscle flap from the intercostal muscle to protect the anastomosis. The patient then had a regular post-surgical course. A chest CT with contrast enhancement and gastrografin after 10 days from surgery (<xref rid="gr3" ref-type="fig">Fig. 3</xref>) showed no spreading of contrast.) showed no spreading of contrast.Fig. 3Postoperative chest CT with gastrografin showed the gastric tubulization (arrow).Fig. 3
